subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Federal vs. State Responsibility for Environmental Policy
stimulus: Environmental regulation is a complex policy area that involves both the states and the federal government. Develop an argument that takes a position on whether the federal government should be primarily responsible for managing environmental policy or if it should be the responsibility of the states.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Brutus 1, The Federalist 10, The Preamble of the United States Constitution.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning about whether the federal government or states should be primarily responsible for environmental policy.

Examples that earn the point: 'State governments are better at making environmental policy because they are closer to the people.' OR 'The federal government is better at making environmental policy because environmental issues in one state can impact other states.' OR 'The federal government is better at making environmental policy because the federal government has more resources.'
parts.scoring_notes: Restating the prompt does not earn the point. Generic statements about federalism without taking a position do not earn the point. The thesis must take a clear position and establish a line of reasoning.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): Uses one piece of specific and relevant evidence to support the claim (requires Row A point).

3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Uses two pieces of specific and relevant evidence supporting the claim, one from a listed foundational document (Brutus 1, Federalist 10, or Preamble) (requires Row A point).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'The federal government can make treaties on environmental issues.' 'The necessary and proper clause allows the federal government to take action.' 'Policy diffusion/laboratories of democracy allow state innovation.' 'Brutus I opposes giving the national government more power over the states.' 'The Preamble's clause to promote general welfare connects to addressing the environment.' 'Federalist 10 says competing interests within a large republic require federal government to mediate.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from Brutus 1, Federalist 10, or the Preamble.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Explains the relationship between evidence and the claim or thesis.

Examples that earn the point: 'Brutus I would support state control because too much federal power will curtail liberty/fail to address local concerns.' 'Because the environment is central to promoting general welfare through health and well-being, the federal government should address it.' 'Competing interests within a large republic require federal government mediation, which can improve policy overall.' 'The necessary and proper clause allows the federal government to take action, making policy uniform and expedient.' 'Policy diffusion allows state innovation to spread to other states or the federal government.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Listing evidence without explaining the connection to the claim does not earn this point.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must both describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it.

Examples that earn the point: 'Some people say that the federal government is better, but federal policies are one-size-fits-all and do not always meet local needs.' 'While some argue that states should control environmental policy, the federal government has better resources/mediates competition/can interact with foreign nations on the issue.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Restating the opposite of the thesis does not earn the point. Must describe an alternate perspective AND engage with it (not just acknowledge it).
